entry.title: How to derive dynamic text label in CDS view with value lookup
entry.content:
Background:

Recently I got a requirement to create an account payable aging report which contains dynamic text based on user input, for example, user input aging interval as: 30, 60, 90. in the output of the report, the required report format will be:

CustomerDue in 30 daysDue in 31-60 daysDue in 61-90 daysDue 91+ days
1001$300$600$900$1200

In the traditional SAP BW world, this can be easily achieved by Text variable, however in SAP CDS view, dynamic text is not available in early versions. In my current system version, the version is ABAP 7.58, and it supports this annotation very well.

Solution:

So instead of going this complex scenario, I will use a simple SAP data source:  SFLIGHT to achieve the similar requirement.

Scenario explanation:

There will be only one base measure called Airfare, based on the user input: number of Days X, the output will show the AirFare, X Days AirFare, X+1 Days AirFare, the highlighted two columns text will be dynamically derived based on the user input

Year MonthAirFare10 Days AirFare11 Days AirFare
02.2023$300$600$900

This is how the end to end implementation work look like

  • Define a helper view: this is to return the day + 1

  • Define cube level data model, with base fare, base fare * input days, base fare * input days + 1, the measure if purely for distingush the different dollar value

  • Comes with the fun part, in the query view, we define the parameter which populate the derived dayPlus1 using lookup view which defined in first step:

  • In the measure column, wewill appy the dynamic text using annotation: @Consumption.dynamicLabel:

Now Let's run the CDS view from BW query, as you can see user will be prompt to fill input days (default value is 10):

The measure column will derive the text dynamically based on user input and helper view:

 

With the help to combine annotation @Consumption.dynamicLabel and @Consumption.derivation.lookupEntity, we will be able to achieve the same requirement in the BW world by using Text variable and custom exit. 

I hope this blog give you some help with the how dynamic text can be implemented using SAP CDS view, if you have any questions, please feel free to leave a message.

entry.title: CDS : How to define default Key Display in Analytical queries
entry.content:
This blog explains how to define default key display settings in analytical queries, ensuring user-friendly key visualization. Many Hierarchy Nodes and Dimensions have compounded keys that are shown as a long-concatenated keys during runtime. Sometimes its not needed to see the concatenated long key. To enhance readability, @AnalyticsDetails.query.keyDisplay annotation is use to specify the default display behaviour for key.

Annotation: @AnalyticsDetails.query.keyDisplay

Accepted Values:

  • #NOT_COMPOUND: Displays the key as a non-compounded characteristic value.
  • #COMPOUND: Displays the key as a compounded characteristic value (concatenated long key).
  • #CONTEXT_DEPENDENT: Displays the key based on its uniqueness. If the compounded key is unique due to drill-down selection, it will be shown as non-compounded

Example

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_ALLOWED
@EndUserText.label: 'Query: Simple Example for Key display'
define transient view entity ZLR_PC_KEY_DISP 
provider contract analytical_query
as projection on ZOQ_FLIGHT {

  
  @AnalyticsDetails.query.axis: #ROWS
  carrid,
  
  @AnalyticsDetails.query: {
      axis: #ROWS,
    totals: #SHOW
  }
  @EndUserText.label: 'ConnectionID Not Compound'
  @AnalyticsDetails.query.keyDisplay: #NOT_COMPOUND
  connid,
  
  @AnalyticsDetails.query.axis: #FREE
  @analytics.hidden: true
  currency,
   
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'currency'
  @EndUserText.label: 'Booking total 2019'
  paymentsum

 
}
where flyear =  '2019'
  and currency = 'EUR'

Output

Release Info

This feature is available from Cloud release 2508

SAP Note: 3600690 - CDS: query display key settings

entry.title: Simple understanding of Hierarchies in SAP S/4HANA Embedded Analytics– Part 1 (Basics)
entry.content:
Accurate CDS hierarchy modeling and understanding is critical for financial reporting and operational analytics. Without well-defined hierarchies, drill-down analysis and aggregated insights become challenging. This blog demystifies hierarchy concepts in SAP S/4HANA Embedded Analytics and shows how they power SAC,Multidimensiona Reports , AFO and Datasphere scenarios.This blog will give you the basic understanding of Hierarchies in CDS Views (S4 Hana)

What This Blog Covers

  • Common hierarchy terminologies
  • Types of hierarchies in CDS Views
  • Standard hierarchies in S/4HANA
  • Why custom hierarchies matter


1. Key Terminologies-

Before diving into implementation, let’s clarify the basics:

  • Root Node : Top-most node in the hierarchy. Has No parent. Signifies the Hierarchy Structure and contains the Hierarchy metadata. A hierarchy can have several root nodes.
  • Hierarchy Node : Represents a group or element in the hierarchy.
  • Parent Node : A node that has child nodes beneath it.
  • Child Node : Belongs to a parent node.
  • Leaf Node : Lowest node in the hierarchy (end point).
  • Hierarchy Directory : Stores hierarchy definitions.
  • Siblings : Nodes sharing the same parent.
  • Hierarchy Level : Indicates depth of a node in the hierarchy. Levels can be defined as 0,1,2,3 . In S/4 EA change level based hierarchies to Parent child hierarchies .
  • Recursive Hierarchy: Nodes can reference themselves (e.g., Org Structure). Implemented using self-association in CDS.
  • Hierarchy Node Texts: These contain the descriptive names or labels associated with the hierarchy nodes, typically derived from the related master data (for example, the name or description of a General Ledger account or Product Hierarchy Description).
  • Orphan nodes, These are the nodes missing a link to a root. You can choose to ignore them, raise an error, or promote them as alternative roots using annotations like ORPHANS = ROOT

2. Example of a Hierarchy-

Company → Product Category → Product → Variant Shoe Model 1

Example:

  • Company (ABC Corp)  → Root Node -> The top most node in hierarchy (Defined via Hierarchy Directory and Root node has no parent defined)---- To be discussed in details in another Part 2.
  • Product Category (Footwear)  → Child of Company->A Parent Node for Product but this is not the Root node(Since it has Company as a Parent Node).
  • Product (Shoes)  → Child of Product Category->Parent Node for Variant
  • Variant (Shoe Model 1)  → Leaf Node->Lowest Node in the hierarchy

 In SAP: For Cost Center or Profit Center hierarchy, Controlling Area could be the root node.

3. Types of Hierarchies in CDS Views-

In S/4HANA Embedded Analytics, hierarchies are modeled using CDS annotations. Most common type:

  • Parent-Child Hierarchy 🔗 Always recursive. Each node references its parent, forming a tree structure. Example: Cost Center hierarchy where each cost center belongs to a higher-level.

 

  • Level-Based Hierarchy 🔗Not supported in S4 Hana Embedded analytics as Analytical engine supports only Parent Child Hierarchy for drill downs and aggregations.

CDS Annotation Example:

@Hierarchy.parentChild:
{ recurse:          {   parent: 'ParentNode',   child:  'HierarchyNode'   },
  siblingsOrder:    {   by: 'SequenceNumber',    direction: 'ASC'   },
  directory:        '_Hierarchy' }

4. Standard Hierarchies in S/4HANA-

SAP delivers several predefined hierarchies:

  • Cost Center Hierarchy – For controlling and cost reporting
  • Profit Center Hierarchy – For profitability analysis
  • GL Account Hierarchy – For financial statements
  • Product Hierarchy – For material classification
  • EHS Location Hierarchies – For Environment, Health & Safety compliance

5. Custom Hierarchies-

Business requirements often demand custom hierarchies:

  • Example: A sales region hierarchy combining geography and product lines.
  • Implemented using CDS Views by developing a relation between Dimension Views and Hierarchy View.

6. Why Hierarchies Matter?

  • Enable drill-down reporting in SAC or Fiori Multidimensional Reports/AFO.
  • Support aggregations at multiple levels
  • Provide flexibility for planning and analytics

7. Technical Details: Where Hierarchies Are Stored-

  • HRRP_NODE_N Table: Stores Parent Node info, hierarchy node level details, and relationships between nodes(Example where GL Account/Profit Center/Cost Center Hierarchy details are stored).
  • HRRP_DIR_N Table: Stores Hierarchy Directory info (name, version, metadata).

👉When you activate or create a hierarchy (standard or custom), these tables are updated.

  • HRRP_NODE_N = Node-level details (Parent-Child relationships)
  • HRRP_DIR_N = Directory-level details (Hierarchy header info)

8. Hierarchy Components & Annotations-

  • A well-defined hierarchy in CDS has multiple views:

Source(Master Data) View: Provides key fields and relationships (Eg GL Account/Cost Center/Product related details).
Hierarchy View: Contains the relationships and parent–child links. Eg - Hierarchy(Child) Node, Parent Node, Node levels etc.
Directory View: Provides metadata about hierarchy variants -- Optional.
Text Views for labeling nodes and directory entries . Eg: GL account and it's text/ Cost center and it's text etc.

Proper annotations (@Hierarchy.parentChild, @ObjectModel.dataCategory: #HIERARCHY) control recursion, sibling order, orphan handling, and directory association

Notes:

  • Parent–Child Hierarchy: Supported in CDS for analytics; recursive structure.
  • Level-Based Hierarchy: Not supported in CDS for analytical consumption (only parent–child works for drill-down).
  • Orphan nodes = Nodes without a parent.
  • A Parent Node can have multiple Hierarchy Nodes/Child nodes but a hierarchy node cannot have multiple parents
  • A node can appear under different parents in different hierarchy versions, not in the same hierarchy( It usually refers to alternate hierarchy versions or variants, not simultaneous parents in the same hierarchy. For example:

Hierarchy Variant/Version 1: Region → Country → City

Hierarchy Variant/Version 2: Sales Channel → Country → City.This is managed via Hierarchy Directory.

  • A CDS hierarchy cannot serve as the underlying source for building another CDS hierarchy.

Next Article (Part 2)

  • How to activate Standard hierarchies and develop Custom Hierarchies in CDS Views (S4 Hana EA).
  • How to consume hierarchies in SAC

entry.title: Extending an SAP Sample Sankey Custom Widget in SAP Analytics Cloud to Deliver Top-N Hierarchical In
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) does not provide a native, built-in Sankey chart as part of its standard visualization catalog. As a result, Sankey-style visualizations in SAC are typically implemented using custom widgets.

In mid-2024, while working on an enterprise analytics engagement, I encountered a requirement to visualize:

  • Top N parent entities (for example, service providers)
  • And, for each parent, the Top M child entities independently ranked per parent (for example, services offered)

All of this needed to be displayed within a single Sankey visualization, enabling users to immediately understand concentration and distribution patterns without navigating multiple charts.

To address this requirement, I explored SAC’s Custom Widget framework and extended an existing SAP-provided sample Sankey custom widget into a production-ready solution.

I have intentionally included the development and deployment steps in detail so that consultants or developers who are completely new to Sankey custom widgets in SAP Analytics Cloud can deploy and validate the solution end-to-end by following this blog.

SAP Sample Sankey Custom Widget as the Starting Point

Since SAP Analytics Cloud does not include a native Sankey chart, I referred to a sample Sankey custom widget implementation shared by SAP through the SAP Community. This SAP-provided sample demonstrates how a Sankey diagram can be rendered using the SAC Custom Widget framework and served as a technical starting point for my work.

The SAP sample was very helpful as a technical reference, as it illustrated:

  • Custom widget structure and lifecycle
  • Binding SAC model data to a widget
  • Rendering a Sankey diagram using JavaScript

During mid-2024, while actively working on this requirement, I engaged in the comments section of that SAP Community blog (SAP Analytics Cloud: Custom Widget & Widget Add-Ons Samples Preview) under my SAP Community user@GovindaRaoBanothu45 , discussing feasibility and implementation boundaries.

While the sample clarified the basic rendering approach, it also highlighted functional gaps when applied to enterprise-scale analytical requirements.

Why the SAP Sample Sankey Widget Was Not Sufficient

The SAP-provided Sankey custom widget was intentionally designed as a generic sample. Its behavior was to render all nodes and all links returned by the bound dataset.

However, the sample widget did not support the enterprise analytical requirement I was addressing, specifically:

  • No Top-N filtering at the parent level
  • No independent ranking of child nodes per parent
  • No data trimming prior to Sankey rendering
  • Limited usability for large, real-world datasets

As a result, while the SAP sample demonstrated how to render a Sankey chart, it did not meet the analytical or usability needs of an enterprise dashboard.

This gap required extending and enhancing the SAP sample Sankey custom widget with additional, original logic to reshape the dataset before rendering.

High-Level Architecture of the Extended Sankey Widget

The Sankey rendering mechanism remains aligned with the SAP sample, while the data-transformation layer represents the core extension.

Development Environment and Prerequisites

Prerequisites

To get started, ensure that you have:

  • Access to SAP Analytics Cloud with Optimized Design Experience enabled
  • Node.js installed on your local machine
  • Visual Studio Code (VS Code) installed
  • Basic understanding of JavaScript and JSON

Development Environment Setup

Install Node.js

  1. Download Node.js from: https://nodejs.org
  2. Verify installation:
node -v
npm -v

Install Visual Studio Code

  1. Download VS Code from: https://code.visualstudio.com
  2. Use VS Code as the primary editor for developing and maintaining the custom widget files.

Set Up the Project Folder

  1. Create a project folder (for example, SankeySAPcode).
  2. Open the folder in Visual Studio Code.
  3. Create the following files:
  • main.js – core widget logic and Sankey rendering
  • index.json – metadata and resource configuration
  • hash256.js – Node.js script for integrity key generation

Sankey Custom Widget – Standard Project Structure

For the standard Sankey implementation, I created a dedicated project folder (for example, SankeySAPcode or any relevant project name). This folder contains all the supporting and required files needed to build and deploy the Sankey custom widget in SAP Analytics Cloud.

The project folder includes:

  • main.js
    The web component JavaScript file that contains the core widget logic and Sankey rendering code (mandatory).
  • index.json
    The metadata file that defines the custom widget, its properties, and resource references (mandatory).
  • sankeyChartStyling.js
    An optional styling component used to expose styling options in the widget’s styling panel (optional).
  • build/ folder
    Contains the helper script hash256.js, which is used to generate SHA-256 integrity keys for the JavaScript files.

Note: Styling files (Styling.js, sankeyChartStyling.js, or xxxxStyling.js) are optional and may not be required for all custom widgets.
However, the web component JavaScript file (main.js or equivalent) and the metadata file (index.json) are mandatory for every SAP Analytics Cloud custom widget. The build folder is included purely for maintainability and ease of understanding.
Technically, the hash256.js file can also be placed directly in the main project folder. Including it in a separate subfolder is optional and does not impact deployment, as long as the final ZIP file complies with SAP Analytics Cloud’s upload requirements.

Creating Integrity Keys and Deploying the Code

SAP Analytics Cloud supports SHA-256 integrity validation to ensure uploaded JavaScript resources are not modified.

Generating Integrity Keys

  1. Open a Node.js command prompt
  2. Navigate to the folder containing hash256.js
  3. Execute the script

Example output:

  • main.js: sha256-6134FFLCQYpH7x8gx29H1xJyRbitpf/guj5UGKhJ25Q=
  • sankeyChartStyling.js:  sha256-AR0uDO+9h8nu9EyJotSinZ2APDWD5Ja2JmdFcZCH1Xo=

Note: If additional JavaScript resource files are included, their corresponding sha256- integrity keys must also be added to the index.json file under the resources section.

Similarly, the file names must be updated in hash256.js before generating integrity keys for those files.

Core Enhancement: Top-N Parent and Top-M Child Logic

The following snippets are representative excerpts from the working implementation.
Full scripts are intentionally not published, as this widget was delivered as part of an enterprise engagement.

In the widget, the SAC data binding is first normalized into an array (data) containing parent node, child node, and measure value fields.

Step 1: Aggregate Measure by Parent

const parentTotals = {};
data.forEach(row => {
  parentTotals[row.parentNode] =
    (parentTotals[row.parentNode] || 0) + Number(row.measureValue);
});

Step 2: Rank and Retain Top-N Parents

const topParents = Object.entries(parentTotals)
  .sort((a, b) => b[1] - a[1])
  .slice(0, TOP_N_PARENT_NODES)
  .map(entry => entry[0]);

Step 3: Rank and Retain Top-M Children per Parent

const filteredLinks = [];
topParents.forEach(parent => {
  const children = data
    .filter(row => row.parentNode === parent)
    .sort((a, b) => b.measureValue - a.measureValue)
    .slice(0, TOP_N_CHILD_NODES);

  children.forEach(child => {
    filteredLinks.push({
      source: parent,
      target: child.childNode,
      value:  child.measureValue
    });
  });
});

Step 4: Rebuild Nodes

const nodes = [...new Set(
  filteredLinks.flatMap(l => [l.source, l.target])
)].map(name => ({ name }));

Step 5: Render Sankey

sankey.nodes(nodes).links(filteredLinks).layout(32);

The rendering follows the SAP sample approach; the innovation lies in the data-transformation logic.

Once all required files and script updates are ready, let’s deploy the custom widget into SAP Analytics Cloud.

Deploy the Custom Widget

  1. Create a ZIP File:
    • Include main.js, index.json, and optional styling files in a .zip file.
  2. Upload to SAP Analytics Cloud:
    • Navigate to the Custom Widgets section in SAC.
    • Upload the .json and .zip files.
  3. Test the Widget:
    • Add the custom widget to a story and configure data sources.

Select the JSON metadata file from your local system.

Next, select the resource file that is .zip file of main.js, index.json and styling.js or any other file related to this chart.

Now you can the widget in "Custom Widgets" section.

Observe the warning messages below with and/without integrity checks in the list of custom widgets

We have done custom widget deployment and will use the same in stories. Let's see how to access this in story 2.0.

 

From the builder panel, select your data model, set measure, set a hierarchical dimension and edit the default colors of the different depths from the Styling panel.

Below screenshot show the usage of the Styling.JS file.

In case the java script files get modified / tampered you get the below error while creating chart using custom widget in SAC story.

Validated Sankey Variants  - Let's validate 3 different scenarios

  • SAP sample Sankey (baseline):

                          

  • Top 1 parent + Top 5 child nodes:
    • This is the custom code and the main.js file is completely different than standard code with the key components are the below conditions below.

                        

                       Result of the above changes,

                    

  • Top 3 parents + Top 5 children:

                          

                        Result of the above changes,

                       

This incremental approach helped validate correctness before applying the logic to larger Top-N configurations.

Acknowledgment

I would like to thank @marouferchichi for actively engaging in the SAP Community blog comments and helping clarify the capabilities and boundaries of the sample Sankey custom widget.

The final implementation described here represents my independent extension and delivery of that sample to meet enterprise-level analytical requirements.

Conclusion

This blog demonstrates how a SAP sample Sankey custom widget can be extended into a production-grade Top-N hierarchical Sankey visualization using SAP Analytics Cloud’s Custom Widget framework.

For similar enterprise scenarios, this approach provides a reusable and scalable pattern.

entry.title: Authorization setup for SAC Import Model from S4 ABAP CDS Views
entry.content:
Purpose of this document

The purpose of this blog is to explain how to implement data-level authorization in SAP Analytics Cloud Stories when:

  • Data is extracted from SAP S/4HANA CDS Views
  • The connection type is Import Model
  • Authorization must be enforced based on Company Code and Plant

This approach ensures that users can access only the data they are authorized to see, even though the data is physically stored in SAC.

 

Business Use Case

In many finance and controlling scenarios:

  • A single SAC Story is shared across multiple business users
  • Users belong to different Company Codes and Plant
  • Data is periodically imported from SAP S/4HANA into SAC

Since Import Models store data in SAC, runtime authorisation from S/4HANA (CDS DCLs) is not applied. Therefore, authorization must be designed and enforced within SAC.

 

Authorization Concept for Import Models

When working with SAC Import Models, authorization is handled as follows:

Layer

Responsibility

SAP S/4HANA CDS

Defines which data is extracted

SAC Model

Enforces row-level data authorization

SAC Story

Automatically respects model-level security

Key points to note:
  • CDS DCLs are evaluated only at data extraction time
  • SAC Data Access Control (DAC) governs user visibility
  • Story designers do not need to manage authorization logic
Example Used in This Blog

The same example is used consistently throughout the blog.

Attribute

Value

Authorization Dimensions

Company Code, Plant

Sample Company Code

0100

Sample Plant

RA01, RA02, …

SAC Connection Type

Import

Source System

SAP S/4HANA CDS View

 

Step-by-Step Authorization Setup

Step 1: Prepare CDS View in SAP S/4HANA

Ensure that the CDS View contains the fields required for authorization, i.e., Company Code and Plant.

Example CDS Used:

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZCDEMO002V'
@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true
@AbapCatalog.preserveKey: true
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Demo Authorization Consumption VIew'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@Analytics.query: true
@VDM.viewType: #CONSUMPTION
define view ZC_DEMO_AUTH_2
  as select from ZI_DEMO_AUTH_1
{
  PurchaseOrder,
  PurchaseOrderItem,
  PurchaseOrderCategory,
  CompanyCode, // Authorization field
  Plant,       // Authorization Field
  @Semantics.currencyCode: true
  DocumentCurrency,
  @DefaultAggregation: #SUM
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'DocumentCurrency'
  PurOrderNetAmount
}

Important notes:

  • Authorization-relevant fields must be part of the CDS output
  • CDS DCLs are not used for runtime authorization in SAC Import Models

 

Step 2: Create Import Model in SAP Analytics Cloud

  • Navigate to Connections in SAC.
  • Create an SAP S/4HANA Import Connection.
  • Import the CDS View as a Model.

  • At this stage, the data is physically stored in SAP Analytics Cloud.

 

Step 3: Enable Data Access Control (DAC)

  • Open the imported Model
  • Navigate to Settings
  • Navigate to Model Preferences
  • Enable Data Access Control

  • This step activates row-level authorization for the model.

 

Step 4: Mark Authorization-Relevant Dimensions

  • Enable data access control for the required dimensions.
  • Select CompanyCode, and Plant.

  • These dimensions are now evaluated during data access.

 

Step 5: Create Data Access Roles

  • Navigate to Security → Data Access
  • Create a new Data Access Role

  • Provide Role information, Role template, and review before creating. Template helps to create roles fast for general sections like BI Admin or Planner. In this we will choose template as ‘Blank’.

  • After role is created, we will move to ‘Select Model’, choose your import model.

  • Select dimensions required for authorizations and provide values for each.

 

Step 6: Assign Data Access Role to Users

  • Navigate to Security → Roles
  • Select the relevant user
  • Assign Business Role – SAC_ROLE_DEMO_001 to users for Row-level authorization.
  • The user now inherits Company Code and Plant restrictions.

 

Step 7: Validate Authorization in SAC Story

  • Create or open a Story based on the model.
  • Add a table or chart in story for validation.
  • Include Company Code and Plant as dimensions.
  • Authorization is automatically enforced at runtime.

 

Results

After completing the setup:

  • Data visibility is restricted at row level.
  • A single Story can be reused for multiple user groups.
  • No duplication of Stories or Models is required.
  • Authorization is centrally managed in SAC.
  • Import performance remains unchanged.

 

Best Practices

  • Enable Data Access Control immediately after model creation.
  • Use consistent authorization dimensions across models.
  • Follow clear and business-aligned naming conventions for roles.
  • Do not rely on CDS DCLs for Import Model security.
  • Always validate authorization using multiple test users.

 

Conclusion

When working with SAP Analytics Cloud Import Models based on SAP S/4HANA CDS Views, authorization must be implemented within SAC.

By using:

  • Data Access Control
  • Authorization-enabled dimensions
  • Data Access Roles

you can achieve secure, scalable, and reusable Story-level authorization based on Company Code and Plant fully aligned with standard SAP practices.

entry.title: SAP Cloud ERP 2602（S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition ）亮点前瞻：分析报表
entry.content:
最新S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602版本的发布，为分析带来了强大的功能增强，旨在提升可用性、简化工作流程并提供更深刻的业务洞察。这些新功能对于依赖及时准确数据来驱动业务发展的业务分析专家、财务相关业务人员以及决策者尤为重要。下面我们将深入探讨这个版本的重要功能。

  • 新应用：日记账分录分析器（新版本）
  • 新应用： 我的注释
  • 视图浏览器图形化展现
  • 审核手册增强
  • 多维分析增强
  • 【重要】CE2602 弃用的CDS 视图

 

新应用：日记账分录分析器（新版本）

此次发布的一大亮点是全新的日记账分录分析器应用，是旧版日记账分录分析器的后继版本。新版本经过重新设计，深度集成了强大的多维分析（Multidimensional Analysis）框架，为财务业务人员提供可交互、可追溯、可探索的日记账分析工作台。它不再是静态报表，而是一个支持灵活切片、动态分组与深入下钻的分析环境，显著提升对复杂财务关系与异常模式的发现能力。

亮点功能包含：

  • 多维分析原生集成：深度利用 Multidimensional Analysis 框架，支持任意维度的切片、筛选与聚合，使复杂分析在应用内即可完成。
  • UI5 组件与计算度量对话框：内置 UI5 交互组件与计算度量对话框，用户可在应用内即时创建自定义计算度量，无需切换工具，方便回答复杂财务问题。
  • 灵活维度与透视分析：支持按分类账（Ledger）、公司代码、会计年度/期间（Fiscal Year/Period）、总账科目（G/L Account）等任意维度组合查看；并可在行/列层面钻取，以透视表（pivot）方式交叉比较与切换数据视图。
  • 明细可追溯性：从汇总层级可直接下钻到单条日记账分录，查看凭证明细、主数据属性与文本，保证分析结果可核查。
  • 结果排序与补充信息：查询结果按公司代码与总账科目排序，便于查看每个科目的过账明细；支持显示主数据属性与文本等补充上下文信息。
  • 用户体验与发现能力：现代化界面、交互切片、计算度量与透视钻取，将静态报表转为面向发现的多维分析工作台，提升分析效率与决策支持能力。

 

业务与部署相关说明：

  • New Experience Switch：新版支持“新体验切换”（New Experience Switch），如果用户同时有旧版与新版权限，可在两者间切换使用。
  • 授权与 IAM 应用：为使用户访问此应用，需要分配 IAM 应用：Journal Entry Analyzer - New Version (F0956B_TRAN)。注意：底层 IAM 应用默认以“非激活”方式交付，默认不会向用户显示新体验切换，需要管理员激活并分配。我们强烈建议激活并分配，以便让业务用户知晓并切换到新版。
  • 对现有自动化/测试的影响：启用 New Experience Switch 会导致受影响应用中的信息提示气泡自动展开；如果系统中有测试自动化（如 RPA 机器人），此行为可能影响测试结果。请参照相关指南（Tips for Test Automation）调整测试脚本或配置以规避影响。

总结：新版本在 Multidimensional Analysis 的深度集成、UI5 交互组件与计算度量能力、改进的派生逻辑与检核提示等方面都有显著提升，使日记账分录分析更加动态、可追溯且面向发现，有助于财务团队快速洞察与决策。

 

新应用：“我的注释”

本次发布引入了全新的“我的注释”应用，作为管理协作反馈和讨论的集中枢纽，显著提升注释的可见性、可追溯性与处理效率。该应用会实时汇总您在启用注释功能的审核手册应用（Review Booklets）和多维分析（Multidimensional Analysis）应用中创建、复制或回复的注释，便于统一查看与管理。主要功能与亮点包括：

  • 同时查看页注释与单元格注释，集中管理所有评论线程；
  • 支持按常见属性检索与筛选（如注释标题、内容、分类、状态），快速定位需要关注的讨论；
  • 支持复制单元格注释的整个讨论线程，便于复用或在其它上下文中引用；
  • 可从注释一键导航回原始报表，快速回到完整的数据上下文进行核对或跟进；
  • 在应用的 Comments 面板中也可直接跳转到“我的注释”，实现不同入口间的无缝流转；
  • 实时显示用户创建的注释，帮助及时跟踪新回复与变更；
  • 注释按讨论线程组织（而非按版本分散显示），便于沿着一条讨论脉络查看历史与当前状态。

 

视图浏览器图形化展现

试图浏览器应用在本次发布中获得了实用性与可视化两方面的增强，便于开发与业务团队更快理解和定位 CDS 视图及其关联关系。主要改进包括：

  • 图形化视图与依赖关系展示：现在可查看所选 CDS 视图的图形化表示，以及视图之间的依赖链路，直观呈现数据模型结构与上下游关系，便于分析影响面与排查问题。

 

  • Fiori Launchpad 搜索条中的 View Browser 搜索模型：将 View Browser 的搜索能力整合到 Fiori Launchpad 的搜索栏，支持自然语言查询，可以查询并展示 CDS 视图的技术细节、业务上下文、建模模式等信息，并支持组合检索，提升查找效率与可发现性。

这些增强使得从业务语义到技术实现的贯通更顺畅——无论是快速定位某个视图的依赖链，还是通过自然语言快速检索相关建模信息，都能显著加快分析、协作与变更影响评估的速度。

审核手册的增强

审核手册（Review Booklets）在新版本中进行了多项实用性与效率提升，主要亮点如下：

  • 自动应用已保存视图：在 My Views 中保存视图时可勾选“自动应用”，更改筛选器后表格会即时刷新，无需手动点击更新。
  • 更灵活的层级显示：支持仅显示根节点或全部节点、按层级或平展展示、显示指定层级深度、将子节点置于父节点上方/下方，以及合并仅含单一子节点的节点，便于按需展开或收拢分层数据。
  • 导出功能：新增“Download Exported Data”按钮，并在“Export Data to Spreadsheet”对话框中增加多项选项，如：
  1. 可选择后台导出以处理大数据量、直接或后台下载导出的表格。
  2. 可“重复合并单元格的内容”以保证导出表格的可读性。
  3. 可为货币或单位生成独立列，便于后续汇总与分析。
  4. 在启用评论功能的情况下，可一并导出单元格与页面评论；
  5. 选项语义已优化（如将“Show repetitive member names”重命名为“Repeat Content of Merged Cells”），提升导出结果的结构化与可用性。
  • 已忽略的凭证过滤器横幅：界面即时显示哪些过滤器被忽略及其影响，提升报表上下文的透明度与可理解性。

这些改进聚焦于提升分析速度、分层可视化与导出可靠性，特别是导出功能在大数据场景下显著增强了可用性与灵活性。

 

 

审核手册设计器增强

 

2602版本下的审核手册设计器带来更直观、更高效的设计体验，主要亮点如下：

  • 所见即所得（WYSIWYG）保存布局
    • 在预览中直接编辑布局并实时查看效果，支持移除度量/维度/结构、调整行集与列集，完成后选择“Save Layout”并为更改的行集与列集命名，必要时设为默认；保存后这些行列集会出现在表格上方的取值帮助中，便于复用。
    • 可隐藏底部包含“Save Layout”的工具栏以仅查看预览，需时再启用布局编辑模式；同时可在历史记录中查看已保存的布局变更。

 

  • 增强的“生成内容”（Generate Content）功能
    • 在设计器的 General 页或 Analytical Queries 页的 Add Analytical Queries 区，可选择 Generate Content，系统会基于默认查询自动生成业务页面、行集、列集和变量，您只需对生成内容进行微调即可，大幅加速初始报表构建。
    • 支持通过自定义 InA 服务添加分析查询：在取值帮助中选择自定义 InA 服务后，可预览（Display）、删除或对选定查询执行 Generate Content，自动生成相应的页面/行列集/变量，随后继续调整与配置。

多维分析增强

Multidimensional Analysis 在新版本中对导出能力进行了显著增强，既支持更细粒度的数据结构控制，也提供了适应不同数据量与使用场景的导出方式，便于后续分析、归档与共享。主要亮点包括：

  • 导出目标与默认命名：导出到表格或 PDF，可在对话框指定文件名；在 Multidimensional Analysis 中默认使用查询标题，若从 Review Booklet 导出则默认使用页面名称。
  • 层级展开（Expand Hierarchy）：开启后导出文件会将行列中的层级一路展开到叶子节点，适合需要逐行查看明细的场景。
  • 重复合并单元格内容（Repeat Content of Merged Cells）：可将合并单元格中重复的内容填充到每个单元格，提升导出表格的可读性与加工性。
  • 货币/单位独立列：可将货币码或单位导出到独立列，便于按币种或单位筛选与汇总（仅在度量未放在行上时有效）。
  • 导出评论：支持导出单元格与页面评论（前提是已启用评论功能且用户有相应权限）；页面评论在导出表格中以独立列呈现。
  • 两种导出方式可选：
    • 后台导出（推荐大数据量）：异步将导出文件生成到服务器，完成后系统通知并需从服务器下载，避免阻塞前端操作；
    • 直接导出：导出文件直接下载到本地，适合小数据量快速获取。
  • 注意事项与限制：若选择“货币/单位独立列”或启用“导出评论”，则不能使用后台导出；相关选项在对话框中清晰呈现，便于根据数据量与需要选择最优方式。
  • PDF 导出同样支持重复成员名/合并单元格的处理选项，保证在不同输出格式下都能获得结构化且可读的报表。

 

 

【重要】CE2602 弃用的CDS 视图

在CE2602版本中，以下CDS视图将被弃用。

名称

描述

C_RAOPENREVENUEPERPERIODQRY_2

Rmng Perf Oblgn w Time Bands - Query

I_RAOPENREVENUEPERPERIODCUBE_2

Rmng Perf Oblgns wt Time Bands - Cube

I_UNITOFMEASURECOMMERCIALNAME

Unit of Measure Assignment of commercial to internal

I_UNITOFMEASURETECHNICALNAME

Unit of Measure Assignment of technical to internal

                                                                                                                               

用户需及时根据系统内的使用情况，将正在使用的弃用CDS视图更新为其后续版本。

如何及时发现受影响的CDS视图？请参考下方的博文。

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition ： 如何及时发现受影响的项目并采取升级行动？

更多关于 SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition分析报表话题:

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud嵌入式分析业务用例汇总及简介 查看
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud公有云版本中三系统开发扩展性与二系统关键用户扩展性报表开发对比 查看

entry.title: Learn How to Build Analytical Queries Using ABAP CDS
entry.content:
The data foundation for SAP S/4HANA embedded analytics is the Virtual Data Model (VDM). The VDM is built using ABAP CDS views that form a stack. The stack starts at the bottom with dimension and fact CDS views reading from ABAP tables. The next layer is the cube that combine the dimensions and measures. Finally we have the analytical queries that are built using a special type of ABAP CDS view called a CDS analytical projection view and they sit at the top layer.

SAP provide thousands of CDS views for all the levels of the stack, out-of-the-box and for all application areas. So why would you want to learn how to build them if SAP already provide them?

Firstly, it is helpful to be able to explore the SAP-supplied CDS views and make sense of them. To do this you need to understand the special syntax that is used. Secondly, you may want to develop your own custom CDS views from scratch for consumption by the analytics tools of SAP S/4HANA. And thirdly, SAP Business Data Cloud consumes ABAP CDS views to generate Data Products. So being familiar with this technology is a very useful skill to have in a BDC project.

In the learning journey Discovering SAP S/4HANA embedded analytics we dip our toe in the water and introduce you to the CDS views of the virtual data model. But we don't go into detail.

For those who want to really dig deep into this topic we developed the learning journey: 
Developing Analytical Queries using ABAP CDS 

In this learning journey you will learn how to to create your own analytical queries using ABAP CDS from scratch. There are lots of videos and code snippets that show you how to express your query requirements.

We even provide you with all the code that is used in the learning journey so you can follow along in your own system. 

As well as the learning journey, we provide the following Live Sessions that bring to life various aspects of analytical query building.

Develop Queries using CDS Analytical Projection Views 

Building Cubes using ABAP CDS Views 

Developing Hierarchies using ABAP CDS 

From Cube to Analytical App in 10 minutes Using the ADT Generator 

We highly recommend you attend these planned Live Sessions (or watch the recordings) to help you quickly develop your skills and to ask questions and learn more about planned enhancements.

Developing analytical queries using ABAP CDS is a hot topic. Many customers need help and this is why we created this learning journey. Remember, the virtual data model of SAP S/4HANA depends on ABAP CDS views. Understanding how they work will ensure you are a much sought after member of your SAP project team.

entry.title: What is SAP S/4HANA Embedded Analytics and How Can I Develop My Skills?
entry.content:
SAP's ERP applications have always provided out-of-the-box reports and tools to develop custom reports. If you have been around as long as I have in the SAP world you will remember SAP R/3 and it successor ECC that provided reporting tools such as LIS and CO-PA. Back then SAP also provided a large number of static, ready-made ABAP reports. Reporting was regarded as a separate function to transactional processing. First you work through a transaction, then you look back and evaluate the data you created using reports. There was usually a delay, often overnight, while the transaction data was extracted and prepared then copied into a readable and summarized structure with batch processes having to run before the users could get at the reports.

But today, with the latest evolution of SAP's ERP suite, SAP S/4HANA, reporting has evolved into embedded analytics which provides insights to the business user in real-time and throughout the transactional processes, not just at the end.

Unlike previous tools, there is no redundant copy of the data. All analytics come straight from the live transaction and master data tables. Instead of static reports we now have sophisticated dashboards that provide flexible, ad-hoc drill-downs from a top level KPI down to the individual live transactions. 

Embedded Analytics is an included component of SAP S/4HANA on-premise, private cloud and public cloud.

Embedded Analytics can be broken down into two main components: content and tools.

Content: There are actually two types of content.

1) A Data Model: SAP provide a complete data model covering all business areas. This is the foundation for all analytics and is known as the Virtual Data Model (VDM). It is built using ABAP Core Data Services (CDS) views that provide real-time access to all operational data without redundancy. They are built in a stack so that each view is reusable and they cover master data and transaction data..

2) Analytical Content: SAP provides ready-to-use analytical content such as business KPIs, dashboards and queries for the business user to use right away in their line of business. The analytical content is embedded within the end-to-end business processes. 

Tools:
Embedded Analytics includes developer tools to create brand-new analytical content, such as KPIs, queries, and dashboards. The tools can also be used to extend the SAP-delivered content such as adding extra fields to queries. These tools are mostly graphical tools so they are easy to learn and accessible by the business users who do not have to rely on IT.

A key thing to remember it that the focus of SAP S/4HANA Embedded Analytics is operational analytics within the SAP S/4HANA system.  SAP S/4HANA Embedded Analytics does not combine data outside of S/4HANA. Embedded Analytics is not a data warehouse for collecting historical data, like SAP BW. The idea behind embedded analytics is to provide real-time access to analytics throughout the business processes of SAP S/4HANA so that optimal decisions can be made at the moment of risk or opportunity. Focus on the word embedded which means embedded in the business processes. What this means in reality is that analytical information appears on the same screen as the transaction, right where you need it. For example, imagine you are creating a purchase order for a missing component needed urgently in your assembly plant. You need to find a local, reliable supplier who stocks the component. In this case, the real-time analytics relating to suppliers will appear right in front of you without having to leave the purchase order, to help you immediately choose the best supplier.  

SAP S/4HANA embedded analytics touches all business processes such as order-to-cash, procure-to-pay, hire-to-retire and many more so it is relevant for everyone who works with SAP S/4HANA.

With the buzz around SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC), you might be wondering where S/4HANA embedded analytics fits. Is  S/4HANA embedded analytics replaced by BDC? The answer is no - BDC does not replace S/4HANA embedded analytics.

SAP Business Data Cloud is a data platform that brings all data (SAP and non-SAP) together into a unified storage architecture. Data is piped to BDC from all applications, including SAP S/4HANA. Data is physically copied from S/4HANA to BDC storage and combined with all data across the organization. The focus of S/4HANA embedded analytics is to provide analytics on live operational data (not copied to a central store) that is deeply embedded in the ERP business processes so that it can be used at the time of a transaction. Whereas BDC is a holistic data platform that focuses on combining data from all applications (SAP and non-SAP from all LoBs) in a central data store (data lake). That data is then enriched and used for AI development and strategic analytics. In short, S/4HANA embedded analytics and BDC have different uses cases. you will get a better perspective if you learn a little about SAP BDC.

So back to S/4HANA embedded analytics - how do you get started developing your skills?

You should start by working through the free-of-charge learning journey Discovering SAP S/4HANA embedded analytics 

We also have a instructor-led version of the content if you prefer to learn in a virtual classroom with others. S4H400 - SAP S/4HANA Embedded Analytics Foundation | SAP Training
(Note: there is a fee for this). 

Want to develop skills on a real system and follow along with the course exercises? No problem! Get access to a fully configured SAP S/4HANA practice system and follow the included exercise scripts from the course, or, as Fleetwood Mac sang, you can go your own way and work on your own ideas. (require a subscription to learning.sap.com)

For a deeper dive on selected topics, join the regular Live Sessions where you can interact with the experts. You can also access the recordings of past sessions (requires a subscription to learning.sap.com).
Getting Started with Review Booklets in SAP S/4HANA Embedded Analytics 
Develop Queries using CDS Analytical Projection Views 

And don't forget to ask your questions at any time in this learning community where you have access to the experts who create and deliver the learning content for all data and analytics topics. They are here to support your learning!

Get learning today and build your skills in S/4HANA embedded analytics and add this popular topic to your resume to increase your value in the SAP ecosystem.

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud Go-Live Success: A Comprehensive Checklist for Driving User Adoption
entry.content:
Implementing SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) successfully requires more than just technical deployment—it demands a strategic approach to ensure optimal performance, user adoption, and long-term value. This comprehensive guide outlines the critical areas organizations must address to achieve a successful SAC go-live for both Analytics and Planning scenarios.

The Foundation: Four Pillars of SAC Success

A successful SAC implementation rests on four fundamental pillars that must be carefully evaluated and optimized:

  1. Data Acquisition and Live Connection
  2. Modeling Excellence
  3. Story Design Optimization
  4. Ongoing House Keeping

Let's explore each pillar in detail to understand how they contribute to driving adoption and consumption in your SAC environment.

Pillar 1: Data Acquisition and Live Connection

The foundation of any analytics solution lies in robust data connectivity. For SAC implementations, this means ensuring your infrastructure can handle the demands of real-time analytics and planning scenarios.

Network and Infrastructure Considerations

Your network configuration becomes critical, especially when implementing live connections to source systems. This includes:

  • Device compatibility testing across laptops and mobile devices to ensure consistent user experience
  • Source system capacity evaluation for live scenarios to prevent performance bottlenecks
  • Tenant capacity planning, particularly crucial for import scenarios and planning workloads

End-to-End Connection Validation

A comprehensive review of your SAC connections should cover multiple technical layers:

  • Cloud Connector configuration and optimization
  • Authentication and Single Sign-On (SSO) setup
  • User authorization frameworks
  • Browser behavior testing in realistic business scenarios

These elements work together to create a seamless user experience that encourages adoption rather than creating barriers.

Pillar 2: Modeling Excellence

Effective modeling in SAC requires balancing functionality with performance, ensuring your solution serves actual business needs rather than simply showcasing available data.

Requirement Alignment and Simplification

Before diving into complex modeling scenarios, evaluate whether your requirements align with SAC's standard capabilities. Ask yourself:

  • Do your needs fit SAC's out-of-the-box functionality?
  • Are you creating unnecessary workarounds for complex workflows?
  • Is your commenting and large table handling approach sustainable?

Business-Focused Design Philosophy

The most successful SAC implementations follow a principle of designing for the business goal rather than building from all available data. This approach involves:

  • Keeping models simple and efficient by using only truly necessary data
  • Reducing data volume early in the modeling process
  • Avoiding unnecessary complexity that can hinder maintenance and scaling
  • Designing for maintainability to ensure long-term success

SAC Best Practices Implementation

Following established SAC best practices ensures your solution is secure, reliable, and provides smooth workflows. Key areas include:

  • Proper date handling and hierarchy design
  • Robust access control implementation
  • Comprehensive validation frameworks
  • Optimized planning area configuration

Pillar 3: Story Design Optimization

User experience in SAC stories directly impacts adoption rates. Poor performance or clunky interfaces can quickly derail even the most well-intentioned analytics initiatives.

Script and Event Optimization

Pay special attention to story pages utilizing scripts in critical events:

  • Review "onInitialization" and "onActive" event implementations
  • Eliminate unnecessary widget refreshes that can slow down user interactions
  • Optimize long-running data actions and multi-actions for better performance

Performance Testing and Validation

Comprehensive performance testing should cover both individual and concurrent usage scenarios:

  • Single-user performance optimization to ensure responsive individual experiences
  • High user concurrency testing to validate system behavior under realistic load conditions
  • Realistic scenario and volume testing to identify potential bottlenecks before go-live

Pillar 4: House Keeping and Ongoing Maintenance

Successful SAC implementations require ongoing attention to system health and optimization opportunities.

System Monitoring and Health Checks

Regular monitoring of your SAC environment should include:

  • Homepage System Overview reviews to identify potential issues early
  • Import job failure monitoring to ensure data freshness and reliability
  • Data action performance tracking to identify optimization opportunities
  • Long-running query analysis to prevent user experience degradation

Job Optimization and Scheduling

Efficient job management contributes significantly to overall system performance:

  • Review calendar job cadence regularly
  • Adjust or reduce job frequency where possible without impacting business requirements
  • Balance system resource utilization across different time periods

Driving Adoption Through Excellence

The ultimate goal of following this comprehensive checklist is to create an SAC environment that users want to engage with regularly. When data connections are reliable, models are intuitive, stories perform well, and the system runs smoothly, user adoption naturally follows.

Organizations that systematically address each of these four pillars typically see higher user engagement, better business outcomes, and stronger return on their SAC investment. The key is treating go-live not as a destination, but as the beginning of an ongoing optimization journey.

Next Steps for Your SAC Implementation

As you prepare for your SAC go-live or optimize an existing implementation, consider conducting a comprehensive review across all four pillars. Start with the areas that most directly impact your users' daily experience, then work systematically through the technical infrastructure elements that support those experiences. If you seek expert services and guidance for any of the areas please check our engagement plans and service portfolio.

Remember, successful SAC adoption is built on the foundation of technical excellence, user-centric design, and ongoing operational discipline. By following this checklist and maintaining focus on these core principles, your organization can maximize the value of its SAC investment while driving meaningful business outcomes through enhanced analytics and planning capabilities.

entry.title: S/4HANA CDS View Dependency Navigator
entry.content:
If you need S/4HANA data for your analytical project, or you want to understand S/4HANA logic—whatever your S/4HANA information needs might be—you will need CDS views. But how do you find CDS views? You can use the Virtual Data Model and CDS Views page, which provides some information, or simply use my CDS View Dependency Navigator for a more flexible navigation across all CDS views. If this is what you need, then my new blog is for you.

You start your navigation from a selection screen on which you specify a starting CDS view or DDIC table and the navigation direction:

To navigate along the hierarchy and display source code, you click on the desired CDS view/DDIC table in the Name and Source Code columns, respectively. If at any step of your CDS view dependency navigation you take a wrong turn, no problem—just press the F3 Back button to go back. 

As you can see, the Dependency Navigator displays:

  • Dependencies
  • Navigation Path
  • Source Code
  • Important annotations such as Description, Lifecycle Status, Successor, etc.

With the Navigation Path, you will never get lost or lose what you have found. This is how to read the Navigation Path:

  • CDS views up the hierarchy are on your right, and CDS views/DDIC tables down the hierarchy are on your left.
  • The ← and → arrows indicate the navigation direction.

If the end of the CDS view dependency hierarchy is reached, an informational message will be displayed:

If you navigate down the dependency hierarchy and need to see the table structure, it can be done as well:

What else is worth mentioning is that the Navigator’s dependency scope is limited to only the CDS views/DDIC tables used in the selection and only one level up/down, without any associations. This eliminates unnecessary clutter and makes navigation easier. Compare the ADT Dependency Analyzer with the Dependency Navigator for the P_SalesAnalytics CDS view:

I have to acknowledge that the S/4HANA CDS View Dependency Navigator was inspired by the ABAP CDS View Object Flow Program. With the introduction of CDS entities in S/4HANA 2020 (ABAP 7.55), it became obsolete due to missing information about CDS entities. This is because the ABAP CDS View Object Flow Program relied on CDS view DDIC views (the sqlViewName annotation) to navigate along the CDS view hierarchy, which is missing for CDS entities.

I had to rethink the approach to finding dependencies to support both CDS views and CDS entities. The S/4HANA CDS View Dependency Navigator parses CDS view code. All it takes is some knowledge of CDS view syntax and regular expressions (regex). The only complication I encountered when parsing CDS view annotations is the mix of structured and flattened syntax, but this can be addressed by processing all combinations.

Now you know what to do if you need to add more CDS view annotation attributes to the Dependency Navigator. Here is the Dependency Navigator code—enjoy

entry.title: How to Assign Authorizations for Analytics for Universal Journal
entry.content:
Managing access to financial data requires careful attention to user roles and permissions. In SAP's universal journal, different accountants need different views of the same data—and that's where authorizations come into play.

What Is the Universal Journal?

The universal journal serves as the foundation for integrated accounting. It combines financial and management accounting data into a single chart of accounts, eliminating the need for reconciliation between these areas. This unified approach brings together:

- General Ledger Accounting
- Asset Accounting
- Controlling
- Profitability Analysis (integrated with margin analysis)
- Material Ledger

All data is stored in table ACDOCA, creating one source of truth for your financial information.

Why Authorizations Matter

Even though everyone accesses the same underlying data, business requirements differ by role. A general ledger accountant might need visibility into all company code data for financial statements, while a cost accountant should only see specific cost centers relevant to their area.

Setting Up Access: Business Roles and Business Catalogs

You grant access by assigning business roles to users using the Maintain Business Roles app. These roles combine pre-defined business catalogs with restriction types that control read and write access at the field level. 

Learn how to set up access: Maintain Business Roles

Key Business Roles and Catalogs

Here are some common examples:

- General Ledger Accountant: SAP_FIN_BC_GL* (Template: SAP_BR_GL_ACCOUNTANT)
- Asset Accountant: SAP_FIN_BC_AA* (Template: SAP_BR_AA_ACCOUNTANT)
- Cost Accountant - Overhead: SAP_FIN_BC_OH* (Template: SAP_BR_OVERHEAD_ACCOUNTANT)
- Cost Accountant - Sales: SAP_FIN_BC_SA* (Template: SAP_BR_SALES_ACCOUNTANT)
- Cost Accountant - Inventory: SAP_FIN_BC_IA* (Template: SAP_BR_INVENTORY_ACCOUNTANT)
- Cost Accountant - Production: SAP_FIN_BC_IA* (Template: SAP_BR_PRODN_ACCOUNTANT)

The beauty of this system is its flexibility. You can use the provided templates as starting points or create custom business roles tailored to your organization's specific needs. 

Setting Up Access: Restriction Types

Figure: Restriction Types in the System

 
To further define access to customer data, authorization values are assigned to restriction fields, which are grouped into restriction types. General organizational restriction types are found in the General section of the Maintain Business Roles app and each contains a single restriction field. The combined settings across all restriction types determine the overall authorization granted to the business role and its assigned users.

Configure restriction types consistently across all relevant business catalogs to ensure users can work effectively within their applications.

Learn how to use restriction types: How to Define Authorizations Based on Restrictions

For a detailed list of the restriction types for analytics for the universal journal, see Authorizations for Analytics for Universal Journal .

Further Reading:

Universal Journal
Business Catalogs
Business Role Templates
